name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبُّكَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱئْتِ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠)
```

```english
When your Lord called out to Moses: [saying,] ‘Go to those wrongdoing people, (10)
```

```arabic
قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ أَلَا يَتَّقُونَ (١١)
```

```english
the people of Pharaoh. Will they not be wary [of Allah]?’ (11)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ (١٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! I fear they will impugn me, (12)
```

```arabic
وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِى وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِى فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَـٰرُونَ (١٣)
```

```english
and I will become upset and my tongue will fail me. So send for Aaron [to join me]. (13)
```

```arabic
وَلَهُمْ عَلَىَّ ذَنۢبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ (١٤)
```

```english
Besides, they have a charge against me, and I fear they will kill me.’ (14)
```

```arabic
قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ فَٱذْهَبَا بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘Certainly not! Let both of you go with Our signs: We will indeed be with you, hearing [everything]. (15)
```

```arabic
فَأْتِيَا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
So approach Pharaoh and say, ‘‘We are indeed envoys of the Lord of the worlds (16)
```

```arabic
أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٧)
```

```english
that you let the Children of Israel leave with us.’’ ’ (17)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا وَلَبِثْتَ فِينَا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
He [i.e. Pharaoh] said, ‘Did we not rear you as a child among us, and did you not stay with us for years of your life? (18)
```

```arabic
وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ ٱلَّتِى فَعَلْتَ وَأَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
Then you committed that deed of yours, and you are an ingrate.’ (19)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَآ إِذًا وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٢٠)
```

```english
He said, ‘I did that when I was astray. (20)
```

```arabic
فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِى رَبِّى حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢١)
```

```english
So I fled from you, as I was afraid of you. Then my Lord gave me sound judgement and made me one of the apostles. (21)
```

```arabic
وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَىَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٢٢)
```

```english
That you have enslaved the Children of Israel—is that the favour with which you reproach me?’ (22)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَا رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘And what is ‘‘the Lord of all the worlds?’’ ’ (23)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (٢٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them,—should you have conviction.’ (24)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥٓ أَلَا تَسْتَمِعُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
He said to those who were around him, ‘Don’t you hear?!’ (25)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘Your Lord, and the Lord of your forefathers!’ (26)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٢٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed your messenger, who has been sent to you, is surely crazy!’ (27)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘The Lord of the east and the west and whatever is between them—should you exercise your reason.’ (28)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَئِنِ ٱتَّخَذْتَ إِلَـٰهًا غَيْرِى لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘If you take up any god other than me, I will surely make you a prisoner!’ (29)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَىْءٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٠)
```

```english
He said, ‘What if I bring you something [as an] unmistakable [proof]?’ (30)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِۦٓ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
He said, ‘Then bring it, should you be truthful.’ (31)
```

```arabic
فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (٣٢)
```

```english
Thereat he threw down his staff, and behold, it was a manifest python. (32)
```

```arabic
وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Then he drew out his hand, and behold, it was white and bright to the onlookers. (33)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُۥٓ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
He said to the elite [who stood] around him, ‘This is indeed an expert magician (34)
```

```arabic
يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِۦ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
who seeks to expel you from your land with his magic. So what do you advise?’ (35)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَٱبْعَثْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
They said, ‘Put him and his brother off for a while, and send heralds to the cities (36)
```

```arabic
يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ (٣٧)
```

```english
to bring you every expert magician.’ (37)
```

```arabic
فَجُمِعَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٣٨)
```

```english
So the magicians were gathered for the tryst of a known day, (38)
```

```arabic
وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
and the people were told: ‘Will you all gather?!’ (39)
```

```arabic
لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ ٱلسَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
‘Maybe we will follow the magicians, if they are victors!’ (40)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا۟ لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (٤١)
```

```english
So when the magicians came, they said to Pharaoh, ‘Shall we have a reward if we were to be the victors?’ (41)
```

```arabic
قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٤٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘Of course; and you will be among members of my inner circle.’ (42)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
Moses said to them, ‘Throw down whatever you have to throw!’ (43)
```

```arabic
فَأَلْقَوْا۟ حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
So they threw down their sticks and ropes, and said, ‘By the might of Pharaoh, we shall surely be victorious!’ (44)
```

```arabic
فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
Thereat Moses threw down his staff, and behold, it was swallowing what they had faked. (45)
```

```arabic
فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. (46)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds, (47)
```

```arabic
رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
the Lord of Moses and Aaron.’ (48)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ ۚ لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٩)
```

```english
He [i.e. Pharaoh] said, ‘Do you profess faith in Him before I should permit you? He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic! Soon you will know! I will cut off your hands and feet from opposite sides, and I will crucify you all.’ (49)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَا ضَيْرَ ۖ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ (٥٠)
```

```english
They said, ‘[There is] no harm [in that]! Indeed, we shall return to our Lord. (50)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَـٰيَـٰنَآ أَن كُنَّآ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥١)
```

```english
Indeed we hope our Lord will forgive us our offences for being the first to believe.’ (51)
```

```arabic
۞ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِىٓ إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ (٥٢)
```

```english
Then We revealed to Moses, [saying],‘Set out with My servants at night, for you will be pursued.’ (52)
```

```arabic
فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (٥٣)
```

```english
Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities, (53)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ (٥٤)
```

```english
[proclaiming:] ‘These are indeed a small band. (54)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنَا لَغَآئِظُونَ (٥٥)
```

```english
They have aroused our wrath, (55)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ حَـٰذِرُونَ (٥٦)
```

```english
and we are alert and fully prepared.’ (56)
```

```arabic
فَأَخْرَجْنَـٰهُم مِّن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٥٧)
```

```english
So We took them out of gardens and springs, (57)
```

```arabic
وَكُنُوزٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ (٥٨)
```

```english
and [made them leave behind] treasures and stately homes. (58)
```

```arabic
كَذَٰلِكَ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٥٩)
```

```english
So it was; and We bequeathed them to the Children of Israel. (59)
```

```arabic
فَأَتْبَعُوهُم مُّشْرِقِينَ (٦٠)
```

```english
Then they pursued them at sunrise. (60)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا تَرَٰٓءَا ٱلْجَمْعَانِ قَالَ أَصْحَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
When the two hosts sighted each other, the companions of Moses said, ‘Indeed we have been caught up.’ (61)
```

```arabic
قَالَ كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّ مَعِىَ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ (٦٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘Certainly not! Indeed my Lord is with me. He will guide me.’ (62)
```

```arabic
فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْبَحْرَ ۖ فَٱنفَلَقَ فَكَانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ كَٱلطَّوْدِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٦٣)
```

```english
Thereupon We revealed to Moses: ‘Strike the sea with your staff!’ Whereupon it parted, and each part was as if it were a great mountain. (63)
```

```arabic
وَأَزْلَفْنَا ثَمَّ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٦٤)
```

```english
There, We brought the others near, (64)
```

```arabic
وَأَنجَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (٦٥)
```

```english
and We delivered Moses and all those who were with him. (65)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Then We drowned the rest. (66)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٦٧)
```

```english
There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. (67)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٦٨)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (68)
```

## (Commentary)

The discourse begins by recounting several stories of the past nations and their prophets—Musa, Harun, Ibrahim, Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, and Shu’ayb (peace be upon them)—to demonstrate that the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) are following the same path as those before them and will arrive at the same destination. Most of them will not believe, and thus Allah the Exalted will seize them with punishment in both this world and the Hereafter. The evidence for this is the conclusion of each of these stories with His saying: **وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ * وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** "Most of them were not faithful. Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful," just as the speech recounting the turning away of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) people was concluded at the beginning of the Surah. This is done solely to draw a parallel between one story and another.

All of this is to comfort the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) so that his breast may not be straitened, and so he may know that he is not an unprecedented novelty among the messengers, nor is anything expected from his people other than how the past nations treated their messengers. Within this is also an implicit threat to his people.

This is further supported by the introduction to the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) with His saying: **وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ** "Relate to them the account of Ibrahim."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبُّكَ مُوسَىٰ** — up to His saying — **أَلَا يَتَّقُونَ** "When your Lord called out to Moses... Will they not be God-conscious?" (26:10-11). That is: mention the time when your Lord called out to Musa and sent him with the message to the people of Pharaoh to deliver the Children of Israel, as detailed in Surah Ta-Ha and elsewhere.

His saying: **أَنِ ائْتِ الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** "Go to the wrongdoing people" (26:10) serves as an explanation of the call. Describing them first as "the wrongdoers (الظالمين)" and then identifying them as "the people of Pharaoh (قوم فرعون)" points to the wisdom behind the mission: their wrongdoing through polytheism (الشرك) and their tormenting of the Children of Israel, as mentioned in Surah Ta-Ha: **اذْهَبَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ** "Go both of you to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled" (20:43), until He said: **فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَا إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ** "So approach him and say, 'We are the messengers of your Lord. Let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them'" (20:47).

His saying: **أَلَا يَتَّقُونَ** "Will they not be God-conscious (يتقون)?" is in the third-person form. It is a rebuke in absentia from Allah the Exalted toward them. Its inclusion at the moment of commissioning Musa (peace be upon him) with the message carries the meaning: "Tell them that my Lord rebukes you for abandoning godwariness (التقوى) and says: Will you not be God-conscious?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ** — up to His saying — **فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَارُونَ** "He said, 'My Lord! I fear they will impugn me... so send for Aaron'" (26:12-13). It is stated in *Majma' al-Bayan*: "Fear (الخوف) is the agitation of the soul due to the expectation of harm, and its opposite is security (الأمن), which is the tranquility of the soul regarding the certainty of benefit." End quote. Most often, fear (الخوف) is applied to the perception of evil such that it leads to practical avoidance even if the soul is not agitated, whereas dread (الخشية) refers to the soul being affected by the expectation of evil such that it produces agitation and anxiety. For this reason, Allah negated dread (الخشية) of anyone other than Himself from His prophets, while sometimes affirming fear (الخوف) for them; He said: **وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا اللَّهَ** "and who do not fear anyone except Allah" (33:39), and He said: **وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً** "And if you fear treachery from a people" (8:58).

His saying: **إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ** "I fear they will impugn me" means the people of Pharaoh will attribute lies to me. His saying: **وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِي وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِي** "and my breast will be straitened and my tongue will not be fluent" (26:13). Both verbs are in the indicative mood (مرفوعان) and are coordinated with His saying: **أَخَافُ** "I fear." Thus, the matters he cited as reasons were three: the fear of being called a liar, the straitening of the breast, and the lack of fluency of the tongue. In the recitation of Ya'qub and others, **يَضِيقَ** and **يَنطَلِقَ** are in the subjunctive mood (بالنصب), coordinated with **يُكَذِّبُونِ**. This is more in line with the natural sense of the meaning; according to this, the reason is one: the fear of being called a liar, which results in the straitening of the breast and the lack of fluency of the tongue. This matches what will come in the verse of Surah al-Qasas, which mentions a single reason: the fear of being called a liar.

His saying: **فأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَارُونَ** "so send for Aaron" means: send the Angel of Revelation to Harun so that he may be a helper to me in delivering the message. It is said to one who is struck by a calamity or for whom a matter becomes difficult: "Send for so-and-so," meaning seek aid from him and take him as a helper.

Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: **فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَارُونَ**—is branched from His saying: **إِنِّي أَخَافُ** etc. The mention of the fear of being called a liar, along with the straitening of the breast and the lack of fluency of the tongue, serves as a preamble and introduction to mentioning and requesting the gift of the message for Harun.

He only cited what he cited and requested the message for his brother so that he might be a partner in his affair, a helper and a confirmer of him in the delivery, not out of a desire to flee from bearing the burdens of the message or to seek exemption from it. It is stated in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: "Evidence that the meaning is such—and not that he was making excuses—is the placement of **فَأَرْسِلْ** (so send) between the initial reasons and the fourth reason, namely His saying: **وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنبٌ** (and they have a charge against me) etc. This indicates its connection to them; had it been an excuse, it would have been delayed." End quote.

This is a fine point, and even clearer than it is His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Qasas regarding the story: **قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْسًا فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ * وَأَخِي هَارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِسَانًا فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِيَ رِدْءًا يُصَدِّقُنِي ۖ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ** "He said, 'My Lord! I have killed one of their men, and I fear they will kill me. My brother Aaron is more eloquent than me in speech; so send him with me as a helper to confirm me, for I fear they will impugn me'" (28:34).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ** "And they have a charge against me, and I fear they will kill me" (26:14). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The term **ذنب** (offense/charge) originally refers to taking hold of the tail (ذنب) of a thing. It is said: 'I tailed him' (ذنبته) when I struck his tail. It is used for every act whose consequence is loathsome, in consideration of what results from its outcome." End quote.

In the verse is an allusion to the story of his killing [the Egyptian] (peace be upon him). Its being a "charge (ذنب)" against him is only based on their belief or in consideration of its aforementioned linguistic meaning. As for it being a "sin (ذنب)" in the sense of a disobedience to Allah the Exalted, there is no evidence for that, and a discussion regarding this will reach you in the commentary of Surah al-Qasas, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ فَاذْهَبَا بِآيَاتِنَا ۖ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ** "He said, 'Never! Go, both of you, with Our signs. Indeed We will be with you, listening'" (26:15). **كَلَّا** (Never!) is for deterrence (ردع) and is connected to what he mentioned regarding the fear of being killed. It contains an assurance for him and a comforting of his soul that they will not reach him. As for his request to send for Harun, the response to it is not explicitly mentioned, except that His saying: **فَاذْهَبَا بِآيَاتِنَا** "Go, both of you, with Our signs" is evidence of the granting of his request.

His saying: **فَاذْهَبَا بِآيَاتِنَا** is branched from the deterrence, implying: go to them with Our signs and do not fear.

This was justified by His saying: **إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ** "Indeed We will be with you, listening." The plural pronoun refers to Musa, Harun, and the people to whom they were sent. No weight is given to the opinion of those who say it refers only to Musa and Harun based on the minimum plural being two; for besides being fundamentally flawed, it is not supported by the dual pronouns before and after it, as has been noted.

Listening (الاستماع) is to incline one's ear to speech and talk. It is a metaphor for presence and complete attention to what transpires between them and Pharaoh and his people during the delivery of the message, as He said in the account in Surah Ta-Ha: **لَا تَخَافَا ۖ إِنَّنِي مَعَكُمَا أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ** "Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see" (20:46).

The summary of the meaning is: Never! They have no power to kill you. So go to them with Our signs and do not fear; We are present with you, witnessing you, and attentive to what transpires between you.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأْتِيَا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولَا إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ * أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** "So approach Pharaoh and say, 'We are indeed the messengers of the Lord of all the worlds: Let the Children of Israel go with us'" (26:16-17). This is an explanation of His saying in the previous verse: **فَاذْهَبَا بِآيَاتِنَا**.

His saying: **فَقُولَا إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "and say, 'We are indeed the messengers of the Lord of all the worlds'" is branched from approaching Pharaoh. The use of the word "messenger (رسول)" in the singular form is either in consideration of each of them individually, or in consideration of their message being one—which is their saying: **أَنْ أَرْسِلْ** (Let go...) etc.—or because "messenger (الرسول)" is originally a verbal noun (مصدر), and the rule for it is to be the same for singular and plural. The implied meaning is: "We are the two possessors of the message of the Lord of all the worlds," as has been suggested.

His saying: **أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** "Let the Children of Israel go with us" is an explanation of the message understood from the context. What is meant by "sending them (إرسالهم)" is their release; however, since the objective was for them to return to the Holy Land which Allah had ordained for them—the land of their fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub (peace be upon them)—their release for the purpose of returning there was termed "sending (إرسالاً)" them to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا وَلَبِثْتَ فِينَا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ** "He said, 'Did we not rear you as a child among us, and did you not stay with us for several years of your life?'" (26:18). The interrogation is for rebuking denial (الإنكار التوبيخي). **نُرَبِّكَ** is from rearing/upbringing (التربية), and **الوليد** is the young boy.

When Pharaoh turned toward Musa and Harun and heard their speech, he recognized Musa and addressed him specifically, saying: "Did we not rear you..." etc. His intention was to object to him first regarding his claim of the message, saying: "Are you not the one whom we reared while you were a child, and you stayed among us for many years of your life? We know you by your name and your description, and we have forgotten nothing of your circumstances. So from where did you get this message, when you are the one we know and whose origin we are not ignorant of?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ الَّتِي فَعَلْتَ وَأَنْتَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ** "And you committed that deed of yours which you committed, and you are an ungrateful one" (26:19). The word **فَعْلَة** (deed) with the *fatha* on the *fa* is a noun of instance (بناء مرة) from the verb. Describing the deed with His saying: **الَّتِي فَعَلْتَ** "which you committed" is to indicate its great gravity and its extreme hideousness and atrocity, similar to what is in His saying: **فَغَشِيَهُم مِّنَ الْيَمِّ مَا غَشِيَهُمْ** "and they were overwhelmed by the sea as they were overwhelmed" (20:78). His intention by "this deed" was his (peace be upon him) killing of the Copt.

His saying: **وَأَنْتَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ** "and you are an ungrateful one." The apparent context, as will be pointed out, is that he meant by "disbelief (الكفر)" the ingratitude for favors (كفران النعمة), and that his killing of the Copt and his causing corruption in his land was an ingratitude for his specific favor toward him—namely, his kindness in refraining from killing him like the other newborns of the Children of Israel and rearing him in his house. Rather, it was because he was from the Children of Israel, whom Pharaoh viewed as his slaves and himself as a lord bestowing favors upon them; thus, for one of them to kill a man from his people and cause corruption in the land was a departure from the state of servitude and an ingratitude for his favor.

The summary of his objection alluded to in the two verses is: "You are the one whom we reared as a small child and you stayed among us for years, and you caused corruption in the land by killing a soul, thus showing ingratitude for my favor, while you are one of my Israelite slaves. So from where did this message come to you? And how can you be a messenger when you are this person whom we know?"

By this, it becomes clear that the interpretation of some—that "disbelief (الكفر)" refers to the disbelief that is the opposite of faith (الإيمان), and that the meaning is "and you are of those who disbelieve in my divinity" or "you are of those who disbelieve in Allah according to your own claim, since you mixed with us for years while being upon our creed"—is not sound. Likewise, the opinion of some that the meaning is "and you are of those who are ungrateful for my favor upon you specifically" is also weak.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَا إِذًا وَأَنَا مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ * فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ * وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** "He said, 'I did it then, when I was astray. So I fled from you when I was afraid of you. Then my Lord gave me judgment and made me one of the apostles. As for that favour you taunt me with, [it is] that you have enslaved the Children of Israel'" (26:20-22). The pronoun in **فَعَلْتُهَا** (I did it) refers to the deed. It is apparent that **إِذًا** (then) is detached from being a response or a penalty and conveys the meaning of "at that time," as has been said. **عَبَّدَهُ** (enslaved him) is used when one takes another as a slave for himself.

These three verses are Musa’s (peace be upon him) response to Pharaoh’s objection. Comparing his (peace be upon him) response with Pharaoh’s objection shows that he analyzed Pharaoh’s speech as an attack on his claim of the message from three angles: First, the strangeness and perceived impossibility of his message given that his condition was known—alluded to by his saying: "Did we not rear you...". Second, the vilification of his deed and accusing him of corruption and crime by his saying: "And you committed that deed...". Third, the taunting him with the favor that he was one of his slaves, which is understood from his saying: "and you are an ungrateful one." The nature of what he mentioned in the response required changing the order: he responded first to the second objection, then to the first, then to the third.

So His saying: **فَعَلْتُهَا إِذًا وَأَنَا مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ** "I did it then, when I was astray" is a response to his objection regarding the killing of the Copt, which Pharaoh had magnified by not explicitly naming it, but rather referring to it as "the deed which you committed" to protect the ears from being struck by its name and pained.

Contemplating the text of the response and its counter-objection shows that His saying: **فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْمًا** "So I fled from you when I was afraid of you. Then my Lord gave me judgment" is part of the complete response regarding the killing. Thus, "judgment (الحكم)" stands in contrast to "being astray (الضلال)." It then becomes clear that what is meant by "being astray (الضلال)" is the ignorance (الجهل) that is the opposite of judgment (الحكم). Judgment (الحكم) is the correctness of vision regarding the reality of a matter and the soundness of opinion in applying action to it; it refers back to the true decree concerning the goodness or vileness of an act and the application of action accordingly. This is what the prophets were given. He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ** "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64).

The meaning is: I did it at that time while I was in a state of "being astray (ضلال)"—meaning ignorance of the aspect of benefit in it and the truth that ought to have been followed there. So I proceeded to defend the one who sought my help, not knowing that it would lead to the killing of the man and result in a dire consequence that would necessitate my departure from Egypt, my flight to Midian, and my being away from my homeland for years.

From here, the weakness of the opinion of some becomes clear—that "being astray (الضلال)" means ignorance in the sense of proceeding with an act without regard for the consequences, as in the verse:
*Let no one act ignorantly (يجهلن) against us,*
*Lest we act more ignorantly than the ignorance of the ignorant.*

Likewise, the opinion of others—that "being astray (الضلال)" means love, as the saying of the sons of Ya'qub to their father was interpreted: **تَاللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِي ضَلَالِكَ الْقَدِيمِ** "By Allah, you are indeed in your old error" (12:95), meaning in your old love for Yusuf. Thus, the meaning would be: "I did it then while I was among those who love Allah, not turning away from His love for anything."

As for the first view, it is an admission of crime and disobedience, whereas the verses of Surah al-Qasas explicitly state that Allah, the Glorified, gave him judgment (حكماً) and knowledge (علماً) before the incident of the killing, and this does not coexist with "being astray (الضلال)" in this sense of ignorance.

As for the second view, besides not being supported by the context, it is impossible according to the etiquette of the Quran to name the love of Allah, the Glorified, as "astray (ضلالاً)."

As for the one who says that "being astray (الضلال)" means ignorance in the sense of lack of intent, and that he only did that ignorantly without intending it—for he (peace be upon him) only intended to strike the Copt to discipline him, which then led to what it led to.

And likewise, the one who says that "being astray (الضلال)" means ignorance of the divine laws (الشرائع), as some interpreted His saying: **وَوَجَدَكَ ضَالًّا فَهَدَىٰ** "Did He not find you astray, and guide you?" (93:7).

And likewise, the one who says that "being astray (الضلال)" means forgetfulness (النسيان), as His saying, the Exalted, was interpreted: **أَن تَضِلَّ إِحْدَاهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَاهُمَا الْأُخْرَىٰ** "so that if one of them errs (تضل), the other will remind her" (2:282). And that the meaning is: "I did it forgetting its prohibition," or "forgetting that a blow is something that usually leads to death."

These are views, each of which can be oriented toward what we have previously presented.

His saying: **فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْمًا** "So I fled from you when I was afraid of you. Then my Lord gave me judgment" is branched from the story of the killing. The reason for his fear and flight is what Allah informed of in Surah al-Qasas with His saying: **وَجَاءَ رَجُلٌ مِّنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنَّ الْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ فَاخْرُجْ إِنِّي لَكَ مِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ * فَخَرَجَ مِنْهَا خَائِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ** "A man came from the city’s edge, running. He said, 'O Moses! The elite are indeed conspiring against you to kill you. So leave. I am indeed one of your well-wishers.' So he left it, fearful and vigilant" (28:20-21).

As for judgment (الحكم), what is meant by it—as we have inferred—is the correctness of vision regarding the reality of a matter and the soundness of opinion in acting upon it.

If you say: The explicit text of the verse is that the gift of judgment was after the incident of the killing, while the import of the verses of Surah al-Qasas is that he (peace be upon him) was given judgment before it; He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَاسْتَوَىٰ آتَيْنَاهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ * وَدَخَلَ الْمَدِينَةَ** "When he came of age and became fully grown, We gave him judgment and knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. And he entered the city..." (28:14-15), then He recounted the story and mentioned the killing and the flight.

I say: The word "judgment (حكماً)" appears here and in Surah al-Qasas in the indefinite form, which suggests that each is distinct from the other. It has been mentioned specifically regarding the Torah that it contains judgment; He, the Exalted, said: **وَعِندَهُمُ التَّوْرَاةُ فِيهَا حُكْمُ اللَّهِ** "while they have the Torah, in which is Allah’s judgment" (5:43), and the Torah was revealed after the drowning of Pharaoh and the deliverance of the Children of Israel.

Thus, it is possible to say: Musa (peace be upon him) was given levels of judgment, some above others: before the killing of the Copt, after the flight before returning to Egypt, and after the drowning of Pharaoh. Allah favored him each time with a level of judgment until wisdom was completed for him with the revelation of the Torah. This is, by way of analogy, similar to how some people are granted in their youth a soundness in their primordial nature (الفطرة) such that their disposition rarely inclines toward evil and corruption; then, when they grow up, they are given balance in intellect and excellence in management, which impels them toward acquiring virtues, and thus they are granted the faculty of godwariness (التقوى). The three states are, in reality, of one essence that grows and increases state after state.

It appears from what has preceded that the interpretation of some—that judgment (الحكم) means prophethood (النبوة)—is not sound, as there is no evidence for it from the wording or the context.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned judgment and prophethood in several places in His speech and distinguished between them, such as His saying: **أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** "that Allah should give him the Book, judgment and prophethood" (3:79), and His saying: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** "They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgment and prophethood" (6:89), and His saying: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgment and prophethood" (45:16), among others.

His saying: **وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** "and made me one of the apostles" is a response to the first objection, which was the strangeness and perceived impossibility of his message while they knew him and had witnessed his circumstances when they were rearing him as a child and he stayed among them for years. Its explanation is that their strangeness and perceived impossibility of his message based on their prior knowledge of his condition would only be sound if the message were an acquired matter (أمراً اكتسابياً) that could be surmised or expected to occur through the attainment of its voluntary premises. But the matter is not so; rather, it is a bestowed matter (أمر وهبي) upon which ordinary causes have no effect, and Allah has made him one of the messengers just as He gave him judgment without acquisition. This is what contemplation of the context yields.

As for what they mentioned—that His saying: **أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا** etc. is intended as a taunting with favor upon Musa (peace be upon him) rather than strangeness and perceived impossibility as we mentioned—the verse in itself, though it does not refuse such a reading, the context of the entire response does not support it. This is because it would ruin the context, as it would necessitate making His saying: **وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ** etc. a response to the favor, which does not fit it, and making His saying: **فَعَلْتُهَا إِذًا** etc. a response to the objection regarding the killing, leaving His saying: **وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** as a superfluous addition for which there is no need. Understand this.

His saying: **وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** "As for that favour you taunt me with, [it is] that you have enslaved the Children of Israel" is a response to his taunting him with favor and his rebuking him for being one of his slaves and having been ungrateful for his favor. The explanation of the response is that this which you count as a favor and for whose ingratitude you rebuke me is an authority of oppression and subjugation, since you enslaved the Children of Israel; and enslavement (التعبيد) through oppression and subjugation is not a favor in any sense.

Thus, the sentence is interrogative, intended for denial (الإنكار), and **أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** is an explanation of what was alluded to by His saying: **تِلْكَ** (that). The summary is that what you refer to by your saying: "and you are an ungrateful one"—implying that you have a favor upon me for which I was ungrateful since you were my benefactor and the benefactor of the rest of the Children of Israel (or since you were the benefactor of us, the assembly of the Children of Israel)—is not true. For your being a benefactor is based only on enslavement, and enslavement is oppression, and the guardianship based upon it is also oppression. Far be it that an oppressor should be a benefactor who has a favor upon the one he has enslaved; otherwise, enslavement would be a favor, and it is not a favor. Thus, in His saying: **أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ**, the cause is placed in the position of the effect.

The commentators analyzed Pharaoh’s speech: "Did we not rear you..." etc. into two objections—as we pointed out: taunting him with the favor of rearing him as a child and his ingratitude for the favor, and his causing corruption in the land by killing the Copt. Thus, the matter became difficult for them from two sides—as we pointed out:

First: His saying: **وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** becoming a superfluous addition for which there is no need in the course of the response.

Second: The unsuitability of His saying: **وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** as a response to his taunting Musa (peace be upon him) with the favor of rearing him in his house as a child.

They mentioned several ways to orient this:

Among them: That it is intended as an admission that his rearing of Musa was a favor upon him, while denying that his refraining from enslaving him was a favor, with the interrogative of denial being implied. It is as if he says: "Is that a favor you taunt me with—that you enslaved the Children of Israel and did not enslave me?" This, as you see, involves an assumption for which there is no evidence in the wording nor any allusion.

Among them: That it is a denial of the very basis of the favor upon him because of his enslavement of the Children of Israel. It is as if he says: "Your rearing of me is not a favor to be taunted with, because you enslaved my people and thereby nullified your deed." Thus, His saying: **أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ** etc. is in the position of justifying the denial. This view, though closer to the mind than the previous one, is an incomplete response in meaning, for his enslavement of the Children of Israel does not change the reality of the kindness he showed toward Musa in rearing him as a child.

Among them: That the meaning is that this favor of rearing which you taunt me with was only caused by your oppression of the Children of Israel through their enslavement, which compelled my mother to cast me into the sea, whereupon you took me and reared me. Since this rearing was caused by your oppression through enslavement, it is not a favor. This is the case, but the difficulty lies in deriving this meaning from the wording of the verse.

Among them: That the one who reared me was my mother and others from the Children of Israel whom you enslaved and then commanded, so they reared me. Thus, this rearing is not a favor from you that you taunt me with, because it resulted from oppressive enslavement. This view is even further from the wording of the verse than the previous one.

Among them: That this is an admission from him (peace be upon him) of Pharaoh’s favor upon him, and the meaning is: "And that rearing is a favor from you that you taunt me with, in that you enslaved the Children of Israel and left off enslaving me." You are well aware that there is no evidence for what he assumed in his saying: "and left off enslaving me."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَا رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ** — up to His saying — **مِنَ الْمَسْجُونِينَ** "Pharaoh said, 'And what is the Lord of all the worlds?'... [I will surely make you] one of the imprisoned" (26:23-29). When Pharaoh spoke to Musa (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of his message, attacking it, and received a response that silenced him, he began to speak to him regarding the One who sent him. Musa had informed him that the One who sent him is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), so Pharaoh questioned him about it and sought clarification by saying: "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" through seven verses.

Clarifying the intent behind them depends on remembering the principles of the pagan (الوثنية) doctrines regarding the matter of Lordship (الربوبية), which have been alluded to repeatedly throughout the previous discussions of this book.

These people believe that the existence of things terminates at a Creator who is the Necessary Being (واجب الوجود), who is One and has no partner in His necessity of existence. He is too sublime to be limited by any boundary in His existence and too great to be encompassed by understanding or reached by perception. Therefore, it is not permissible to worship Him, because worship (العبادة) is a type of turning toward the worshipped, and turning is a form of perception.

For this very reason, they turned away from worshipping Him and seeking proximity to Him, toward seeking proximity to things from His creation that possess noble existences—be they of light or fire—which are near to Him, annihilated in Him. These include angels, jinn, and the saints among humans who have freed themselves from the taints of matter, becoming annihilated in the Divinity (اللاهوت) and subsisting through it. Among them were the great kings, or some of them, according to the ancient pagans, and among them were Pharaoh and Musa. In short, they used to worship them by worshipping their idols so that they might bring them near to Allah in proximity and intercede (يشفعوا) for them—meaning that they would pour upon them the good that overflows from them, as in the case of the angels, or that they would not strike them with the evil that emanates from them, as in the case of the jinn. For each of these worshipped beings is responsible for the management (تدبير) of one of the universal affairs of the world, such as love, hate, peace, war, prosperity, and others, or a region of its regions, such as the heaven, the earth, man, and the like.

Thus, there are lords (أرباب) and gods (آلهة), each of whom disposes in the world whose management returns to him, such as the god of the world of the earth and the god of the world of the heaven. These are the angels, the jinn, and the saints of humanity. And the God of the world of gods is Allah, the Glorified; He is the God of gods and the Lord of lords.

Once you know what we have mentioned, it becomes clear to you that there is no sound meaning for the phrase "Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين)" according to the pagans, given their principles. For if what is meant by it is some of these noble contingent beings (الموجودات الشريفة الممكنة) themselves, then such a being is the lord of one world among the worlds of creation—the world in which he directly disposes, such as the world of the heaven or the world of the earth, for example. And if what is meant by it is Allah, the Glorified, then He is the Lord of the world of lords and the God of the world of gods only, not of all the worlds. And if something other than these two categories—the Lord who is the Necessary Being and the lords who are contingent beings—is meant, then it has no rationally conceivable referent.

Thus, his saying: **قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَا رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "Pharaoh said, 'And what is the Lord of all the worlds?'" is a question from him about the reality (حقيقة) of the Lord of all the worlds. The explanation is that Pharaoh was a pagan who worshipped idols, yet he simultaneously claimed divinity (الألوهية). As for his worship of idols, it is due to His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ** "and [to] abandon you and your gods" (7:127). As for his claim of divinity, it is due to the aforementioned verse and His saying, the Exalted: **فَقَالَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ** "and said, 'I am your exalted lord!'" (79:24).

There is no contradiction in paganism between a thing being a god and a lord, and its being nurtured (مربوباً) by another lord. For Lordship (الربوبية) is independence in the management of something in the world, and it does not contradict contingency (الإمكان) and being nurtured by something else. Every lord according to them is nurtured by another, except for Allah, the Glorified; He is the Lord of lords with no lord above Him, and the God of gods with no god for Him.

Kingship according to paganism was a manifestation of the Divinity (اللاهوت) in some human souls through authority and the influence of command. Thus, kings were worshipped just as the lords of idols were worshipped, as were the heads of households in their homes. Pharaoh was a pagan who worshipped the gods, and he was the king of the Copts, worshipped by his people like the other gods.

So when he heard from Musa and Harun their saying: **إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "We are indeed the messengers of the Lord of all the worlds," he marveled at it, as he could not conceive a realized meaning for it. For if what was meant by it was the Necessary Being, who is Allah, then He is to him the Lord of the world of lords, not of all the worlds. And if what was meant by it was some of the noble contingent beings among the gods, such as some of the angels and others, then he is also to him the lord of one world among the worlds of creation, not of all the worlds. So what is the meaning of "Lord of all the worlds"?

Therefore, he said: **وَمَا رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" thus asking about the reality of the One described by this attribute, insofar as He is described by this attribute. He did not ask about the reality of Allah, the Glorified, for due to his paganism, he was a believer in His existence, submitting to it. He believed, like other pagans, that there is no way to perceive His reality—how could it be otherwise? For it is the foundation of their doctrine upon which they build the worship of other gods and lords, as you have heard.

His saying: **قَالَ رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ** "He said, 'The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, should you have certainty'" (26:24) is Musa’s (peace be upon him) response to his question: "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" It is the predicate of a suppressed subject. The summary of the meaning, based on the correspondence between the question and the response, is: He is the Lord of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, which—by the existence of management (التدبير) within them and its being a single, continuous, and interconnected management—point to the fact that they have one manager (مدبراً)—a Lord—as seen by those who possess certainty (الموقنون) and follow the path of certainty through proof and intuitive experience.

In other words: My meaning by "all the worlds (العالمين)" is the heavens and the earth and what is between them, which point by the single management within them to the fact that they have one managing Lord. And my meaning by "Lord of all the worlds" is that single Lord to whom these point, and this is a certain indication found by the people of certainty who employ proof and intuitive experience.

If you say: Pharaoh only requested from Musa (peace be upon him) that he define for him what this is that he calls "Lord of all the worlds"? And what is His reality? Since it was not rationally conceivable to him, he only asked for a conceptualization (التصور). So what is the meaning of His saying: "should you have certainty (إن كنتم موقنين)," when certainty (اليقين) is a judgmental knowledge (علم تصديقي) upon which conceptualization does not depend at all?

Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) did not bring in his response to Pharaoh anything other than placing the words "the heavens and the earth and what is between them" in the place of the word "all the worlds." Thus, it was an explanation of a collective noun by the names of its individual parts, like explaining "men" by "Zayd, Amr, and Bakr." Ultimately, it provided nothing but the initial conceptualization, and certainty has no effect on that.

I say: It is accepted without doubt that Pharaoh was asking him to conceptualize for him the "Lord of all the worlds." However, Musa changed the phrasing by placing "the heavens and the earth and what is between them" in the place of "all the worlds," which points to the interconnection of some parts with others and the continuity between them such that it leads to the unity of the management occurring within them and the system running over them. Then he qualified it with His saying: "should you have certainty (إن كنتم موقنين)" to indicate that the people of certainty affirm (يصدقون) from that the existence of one manager for all the worlds.

It is as if it were said to him: "What do you mean by the Lord of all the worlds?" and he replied: "I mean by Him what the people of certainty mean when they infer from the interconnection and continuity of management in the worlds of the heavens and the earth and what is between them that all these worlds have one manager and a Lord who has no partner in His Lordship over them. And since they affirm the existence of one Lord for all the worlds, they conceptualize Him in a way, for there is no meaning to affirmation without conceptualization."

In brief: The Lord of all the worlds is He of whose Lordship over all the heavens and the earth and what is between them the certain ones are convinced when they look at them and witness the unity of management within them.

Arguing from the realization of affirmation (التصديق) for the realization of conceptualization (التصور) before it is the strongest argument that can be made that He, the Exalted, is perceived in a way and conceptualized with a correct conceptualization, even if it is impossible to perceive Him in His essence or encompass Him in knowledge.

From all this, it has become clear, first: that the response is only by referring him in his request to what the certain ones conceptualize of Him when they affirm His existence.

Second: that the argument alluded to in the verse is the proof for the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) derived from the unity of management, for that is what is needed against the pagans who claim partners in Lordship.

By this, the error of what some have mentioned becomes clear—that since knowledge of the reality of the Essence was impossible, Musa (peace be upon him) turned from defining the reality by a limit to defining Him, the Exalted, by His attributes, saying: "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them," and alluded by His saying: "should you have certainty" to their indication by their temporal origination (حدوثها) that their Originator is a single Essence, the Necessary Being, with whom nothing else shares in the necessity of existence.

The reason for this being erroneous is what you have known: that the pagans are proponents of the impossibility of knowing the reality and essence of the Essence, and that the Creator is an Essence who is the Necessary Being with whom no other shares in the necessity of existence, and that the gods besides Allah are contingent beings, each of whom is a manager of a direction among the directions of the world, and they are all created by Allah. Thus, what they established regarding the meaning of the verse is of no use in the context of disputation with them.

His saying: **قَالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُ أَلَا تَسْتَمِعُونَ** "He said to those around him, 'Do you not hear?'" (26:25). That is: do you not incline your ears to what Musa is saying? The interrogation is for expressing wonder; he wants them to listen to him so they may marvel at his speech where he claims the message of the Lord of all the worlds, and when asked "What is the Lord of all the worlds?" he repeats the word again and adds nothing to what he began with.

This is a deception (تمويه) from him toward them, by which he intends to cover the truth that shone from Musa’s (peace be upon him) speech. For Musa only said that all the worlds point, by the unity of management that the people of certainty witness within them, to the fact that they have one managing Lord, who is the one you ask me about. Pharaoh, however, interprets his speech as saying: "I am the messenger of the Lord of all the worlds," and when I ask him "What is the Lord of all the worlds?" he answers me that He is the Lord of all the worlds.

From what has preceded, the lack of soundness in their opinion regarding the interpretation of this wonder becomes clear—that his intention was: "I asked him about the Essence and he answered with the attribute." This is because the question is only about the Essence from the perspective of its attribute, as previously explained. Musa did not explain the Essence by the attribute, but rather changed his saying: "Lord of all the worlds" to his saying: "Lord of the heavens and the earth," thus placing his second saying "the heavens and the earth" in the place of his first saying "all the worlds," as if he were hinting that Pharaoh did not understand the meaning of "all the worlds."

His saying: **قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبَائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** "He said, 'Your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers'" (26:26) is Musa’s (peace be upon him) second response. For when he saw Pharaoh’s deception of those around him—having already answered his question "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" by explaining "all the worlds" from the macrocosm (العالم الكبير) such as the heavens and the earth and what is between them—he turned a second time to what would be more explicit in the objective. So he mentioned His Lordship, the Exalted, over the worlds of humanity; for "the world (العالم)" is the group of people or things, so the worlds of man are the groups of those present and those past. Therefore, he said: "Your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers."

For Pharaoh was not in reality defending anything but himself, as he was claiming divinity. He was maneuvering to invalidate the connection of the Lord’s Lordship to him within the context of its connection to all the worlds, because that would necessitate the invalidation of the Lordship of the lords, and he was among them—even if he saw himself as the highest and most important of them, as Allah the Exalted recounted of him: **فَقَالَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ** "and said, 'I am your exalted lord!'" (79:24), and: **وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرِي** "Pharaoh said, 'O elite! I do not know of any god for you other than myself'" (28:38).

It is as if he were saying: "If you mean by the Lord of all the worlds Allah, then He is the Lord of lords and nothing else; and if you mean someone else among the gods, then each of them is a lord of a specific world. So what is the meaning of the Lord of all the worlds?" Musa answered with what summarized that there is nothing in existence but one Lord, and He is the Lord of all the worlds; thus He is your Lord, and He has sent me to you.

The summary of Pharaoh’s deception was that Musa had not answered him with anything since he repeated the wording. So Musa answered him a second time by explicitly stating that the Lord of all the worlds is the Lord of the worlds of humanity, from those present and those past, and by that, his ruse was cut off.

His saying: **قَالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ** "He said, 'Indeed your apostle who has been sent to you is surely crazy'" (26:27) is Pharaoh’s second statement. He called Musa a "messenger" mockingly and sarcastically, and attributed him to those around him to elevate himself from being the one to whom he was sent. He accused him of madness, relying on his (peace be upon him) saying: "Your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers" etc.

It is as if he says: "He is surely crazy because of the disorder in his speech, which reveals the disorder in his intellect. He claims the message of the Lord of all the worlds? So I ask him 'What is the Lord of all the worlds?' and he repeats the wording approximately at first, then explains it as being your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers."

His saying: **قَالَ رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** "He said, 'The Lord of the east and the west and whatever is between them, should you use your intellect'" (26:28). The apparent context is that what is meant by "the east (المشرق)" is the direction of the rising of the sun and the other celestial luminous bodies, and by "the west (المغرب)" the direction in which they set according to the senses, and by "whatever is between them" what is between the two directions. Thus, it encompasses the witnessed world and is equivalent to "the heavens and the earth and what is between them."

Thus, it is a repetition of the meaning of the first response with another presentation, which contains what the first contained of the subtle point of the interconnection and unity of management. For the rising has a connection to the setting, and the east and the west are realized as two ends for a middle between them, just as the heaven has an earth and they have an affair between them. This type of unity accepts nothing but a single, continuous management. And just as every present nation has an existential connection to the past nations—the connection of successors to predecessors—the species is one and the management is one, so the Manager is one.

He replaced his saying in the first response: "should you have certainty (إن كنتم موقنين)" with his saying here: "should you use your intellect (إن كنتم تعقلون)" as an exposure of Pharaoh, who had said to those around him: "Do you not hear?" mocking him and insulting him. Then he accused him a second time of madness and disorder of speech. So he (peace be upon him) pointed by His saying: "should you use your intellect" to the fact that they are the ones deprived of the blessing of intellect and understanding; for if they used their intellect, they would have understood that his first response was not a useless repetition, and it would have sufficed them as a proof for the oneness of the Lord—and that the one who carries out the management of all the worlds of the heavens and the earth and what is between them is one Manager, with no manager besides Him and no Lord other than Him.

It has become clear from what has been mentioned that the verse—meaning His saying: "The Lord of the east..." etc.—is another presentation of His saying in the first response: "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them," and that it is a proof for the unity of the Manager by way of the unity of management. In that is a definition of the Lord of all the worlds as the one Manager to whom the single management in all the worlds points. Indeed, the explanation to which this verse points is clearer because it includes the meaning of the rising and the setting, and their being part of the management is apparent.

They mentioned that the arguments contained in the verses are arguments for the oneness of the Essence of the Necessary Being in Himself and the negation of a partner in the necessity of existence, but the lack of soundness of this has already been established.

His saying: **قَالَ لَئِنِ اتَّخَذْتَ إِلَٰهًا غَيْرِي لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ الْمَسْجُونِينَ** "He said, 'If you take up any god other than me, I will surely make you one of the imprisoned'" (26:29) is a threat from him to Musa (peace be upon him) if he persists in what he says regarding the Lordship of the Lord of all the worlds, claiming that he is a messenger from Him. This is the habit of the ignorant, stubborn person: when he is cut off from argument, he resorts to threats and clings to intimidation.

"Taking up a god other than me" is a metaphor for speaking of the Lordship of the Lord of all the worlds to whom Musa calls. He did not mention Him by name to protect his tongue from uttering His name. He paid no heed to the other gods they used to worship, out of arrogance and haughtiness. It seems that imprisonment was the penalty for those who turned away from him and denied his divinity.

It is apparent that the *lam* in **الْمَسْجُونِينَ** (the imprisoned) is for specification (للعهد). The meaning is: "If you persist in what you say, I will surely place you in the group of those who are in my prison, whose evil state and severe punishment you know." For this reason, he did not turn from this expression to something like "I will surely imprison you," despite its brevity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَيْءٍ مُّبِينٍ** "He said, 'What! Even if I should bring you something manifest?'" (26:30). The speaker is Musa (peace be upon him). What is meant by "something manifest (بشيء مبين)" is something that clarifies and manifests the correctness of his claim; it is the sign of the message (آية الرسالة) which points to the truthfulness of the claim of the message from its claimant. For the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة) only points to the truthfulness of the messenger in his claim of the message; as for the divine gnoses (المعارف الإلهية) to which he calls—such as monotheism (التوحيد) and the Return (المعاد) and what pertains to them—the way to establish them is through demonstrative proof (الحجة البرهانية). The conduct of the prophets in their call proceeded upon this, as discussed in the first volume of the book.

The meaning is: Musa said: "Will you make me one of the imprisoned even if I bring you something that clarifies my truthfulness in what I have claimed of the message?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** "He said, 'Bring it, if you are truthful'" (26:31). The speaker is Pharaoh. He branched his command to bring it upon Musa’s interrogation, which suggested that he claimed to have something manifest. Therefore, he qualified the command to bring it with His saying: "if you are truthful," meaning: if you are truthful that you have something of that sort.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ * وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ بَيْضَاءُ لِلنَّاظِرِينَ** "So he threw down his staff, and behold, it was a manifest python. And he drew out his hand, and behold, it was white to the onlookers" (26:32-33). These are the two signs that were given to Musa on the night of Tur. The python (الثعبان) is the great serpent, and its being "manifest (مبين)" means the appearance of its reality such that it is not doubted. What is meant by "drawing out his hand" is drawing it out from his garment after placing it in it, as in Surah al-Naml, verse 12, and Surah al-Qasas, verse 32.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَسَاحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ * يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ** "He said to the elite around him, 'This is indeed a skilled magician. He desires to expel you from your land by his magic. So what do you advise?'" (26:34-35). The speaker is Pharaoh. He had said to Musa: "Bring it, if you are truthful," hoping that he would bring something in which there was room for opposition and dispute. When he brought that in which there was no room for doubt, he found no way out but to slander him as being a "skilled magician (ساحر عليم)."

Therefore, he followed his accusation of magic with His saying: "He desires to expel you from your land by his magic," to incite them against him and urge them to agree with him on repelling him by any possible means.

His saying: **فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ** "So what do you advise?" Perhaps what is meant by "commanding (الأمر)" is suggesting to him, since the suggester suggests to the one who consults him using the wording of a command. So the meaning is: "If the matter is such, then what do you suggest I treat him with so that I may act upon it?" This is because he viewed himself as their highest lord and viewed them as his slaves, and it would not be appropriate to take "command (الأمر)" in its conventional sense.

This meaning is supported by the fact that He, the Exalted, recounted this speech in another place from the elite themselves, as He said: **قَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَسَاحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ * يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُمْ ۖ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ** "The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, 'This is indeed a skilled magician. He desires to expel you from your land. So what do you advise?'" (7:110-111). It is apparent that what is meant by their "commanding" is their suggesting to Pharaoh that he do such-and-such to them.

It is said: The authority of the miracle overwhelmed and dazed him, so he strayed from his wonder and arrogance, and wretchedness overcame him, so he did not know what to say or how to speak.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَابْعَثْ فِي الْمَدَائِنِ حَاشِرِينَ * يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ** "They said, 'Put him and his brother off [for a while], and send heralds to the cities, to bring you every skilled magician'" (26:36-37). The speakers are the elite around him, who were the nobles of his people. His saying: **أَرْجِهْ** (Put him off) with the *sukun* on the *ha* according to the common recitation is a command from **الإرجاء**, meaning delay. That is: delay Musa and his brother and give them respite, and do not hasten to punish or imprison them or the like, until you oppose their magic with magic like it.

It is also recited as **أَرْجِهِ** with the *kasra* on the *ha* and **أرجئه** with the *hamza* and the *damma* on the *ha*; both are more eloquent than the common recitation, and the meaning is the same in any case.

His saying: **وَابْعَثْ فِي الْمَدَائِنِ حَاشِرِينَ** "and send heralds to the cities." **المدائن** is the plural of **مدينة**, which is the town. **الحاشر** (the herald/gatherer) is from **الحشر**, which is to bring out to a place with agitation. That is: send into the lands a number of your officers and soldiers to gather every skilled magician within them and bring them to you so that you may oppose the two of them with their magic.

The use of the intensive form **سَحَّار** (very skilled magician) instead of **ساحر** (magician) is to point out that there are those who are more knowledgeable than him in the arts of magic and more prolific in practice.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَجُمِعَ السَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقَاتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** "So the magicians were gathered for the tryst of an appointed day" (26:38). It is the Day of Adornment (يوم الزينة) which Musa and Pharaoh agreed to make the appointed time for the confrontation, as in Surah Ta-Ha. Thus, there is conciseness and summary in the speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ * لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ السَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا هُمُ الْغَالِبِينَ** "And the people were asked, 'Will you all gather? Maybe we will follow the magicians if they are the victors'" (26:39-40). The interrogation is to urge and encourage the people to gather.

It is stated in *al-Kashshaf* that the summary of the meaning is: what is meant by "following the magicians" is following them in their religion—and they were ostensibly worshippers of Pharaoh, as appears from the context of the following verses. Their intention by this was only that they should not follow Musa, not the following of the magicians. They only cast their speech in the form of a metaphor to incite the magicians to concern and seriousness in the struggle for victory.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا جَاءَ السَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغَالِبِينَ * قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ** "So when the magicians came, they said to Pharaoh, 'Shall we have a reward if we are the victors?' He said, 'Yes, and you will then be among those who are near [to me]'" (26:41-42). The interrogation carries the meaning of a request. They said: "if we are (إن كنا)" and did not say "when we are (إذا كنا)" the victors, to avoid expressing certainty of victory—as is expressed by their later saying: "By the might of Pharaoh, we shall indeed be the victors"—but rather they cast it in the form of doubt so that it would be more inviting for Pharaoh to grant the reward.

This had its effect, as he granted them a reward and added to it the promise of making them among those near to him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰ أَلْقُوا** — up to His saying — **تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ** "Moses said to them, 'Throw down...' [and behold, it] swallowed what they had faked" (26:43-45). **الحبال** is the plural of **حبل** (rope), and **العصي** is the plural of **عصا** (staff). **اللقف** is to swallow quickly. **ما يأفكون** is from **الإفك**, meaning to turn a thing away from its proper face; magic was called **إفك** because it involves turning a thing from its real form to an imaginary form. The meaning of the verses is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ ساجِدِينَ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ رَبِّ مُوسى وَهارُونَ** — "Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron.’" (26:46-48). This means that when the magicians witnessed the dazzling signs (الآيات الباهرة) they saw, they were so overwhelmed and astonished that they could not restrain themselves from falling prostrate upon the earth before Allah, the Glorified. The term "thrown" (الإلقاء) is used metaphorically for their falling to the ground to indicate their lack of self-control, as if they had been cast down upon the earth.

His saying: **قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds’" (26:47) contains an expression of faith in Allah, the Glorified—a faith of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. This is because, as previously mentioned, acknowledging Him, the Exalted, as the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين) cannot be complete except with monotheism and the negation of any deities besides Him.

His saying: **رَبِّ مُوسى وَهارُونَ** — "the Lord of Moses and Aaron" (26:48) contains an allusion to belief in the message (الرسالة) in addition to monotheism.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "He said, ‘Did you believe in him before I should give you leave? He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic. Soon you will know!’" (26:49) until the end of the verse. The speaker is Pharaoh. The meaning of his saying: **آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ** — "Did you believe in him before I should give you leave?" is: "You believed without permission from me," similar to the usage in His saying, the Exalted: **لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِماتُ رَبِّي** — "the sea would surely be exhausted before the words of my Lord are exhausted" (18:109). It does not imply that permission was possible or expected from him, as some have suggested.

His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ** — "He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic" is another slander (بهتان) he fabricated against Moses (عليه السلام) to turn the hearts of his people, and specifically their elite (الملأ), away from him.

His saying: **فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "Soon you will know!" is a threat against them delivered in an ambiguous (الإبهام) context to indicate that he has no need to specify the punishment, for they shall soon experience it.

His saying: **لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, and I will surely crucify you all" (26:49). Cutting "on opposite sides" (من خلاف) means cutting the right hand with the left foot, or vice versa. Crucifixion (التصليب) is to place the criminal upon a cross. A similar verse has already been discussed in Surah al-A'raf and Surah Ta-Ha.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا لا ضَيْرَ إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ** — "They said, ‘[There is] no harm [in that]! Indeed we shall return to our Lord.’" (26:50). The word **الضير** means harm (الضرر). Their saying: **إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ** — "Indeed we shall return to our Lord" is the justification (تعليل) for their statement "no harm"; meaning: "We shall not be harmed by this punishment with which you threaten us, for we shall remain patient and thereby return to our Lord—and what a noble return that is!"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ لَنا رَبُّنا خَطايانا أَنْ كُنَّا أَوَّلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "Indeed we hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins for our being the first of the believers." (26:51). This is the justification for what is understood from their previous speech—that they do not fear death or being killed, but rather long for the meeting with their Lord. They are saying: "We do not fear anything of your punishment because we return through it to our Lord, and we do not fear the return because we hope our Lord will forgive us our sins due to our being the first to believe in Moses and Aaron, the two messengers of our Lord."

Opening the door to any good (خير) has an effect of goodness that the sound intellect (العقل السليم) does not doubt. Thus, if Allah, the Glorified, were to honor a believer with forgiveness and mercy for his faith, His forgiveness and mercy would not bypass the very first ones to open this door and enter this path.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ** — "We revealed to Moses, [saying], ‘Set out with My servants by night, [for] you will be pursued.’" (26:52). This is the beginning of the narration of the second part of the story, which is the description of the punishment of the House of Pharaoh due to their rejection of the call of Moses and Aaron (عليه السلام). The first part was the message of Moses and Aaron to them and their call to monotheism. The terms **الإسراء** and **السري** refer to traveling by night. The meaning of "My servants" (عبادي) is the Children of Israel, and in this expression, there is a form of honoring them.

His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ** — "you will be pursued" is the justification for the command; meaning: "Travel with them by night so that the House of Pharaoh may pursue you." In this, there is an indication that Allah had a purpose in their pursuit and that therein lay the deliverance of the Children of Israel. He stated this explicitly in His saying: **فَأَسْرِ بِعِبادِي لَيْلاً إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ وَاتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً إِنَّهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُغْرَقُونَ** — "‘Set out with My servants by night, [for] you will be pursued. And leave the sea parted: they are a host that is to be drowned.’" (44:23-24).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي الْمَدائِنِ حاشِرِينَ** — "Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities" (26:53) until His saying: **ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ** — "Then We drowned the rest" (26:66). This is the story of the drowning of the House of Pharaoh and the salvation of the Children of Israel in fourteen verses. The discourse is concise, omitting some stages of the story because they are evident from the context, such as the departure of Moses and the Children of Israel by night from Egypt, as indicated by His saying: **أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي** — "Set out with My servants by night."

Thus, He, the Exalted, said: **فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ** — "Then Pharaoh sent"; meaning: Moses traveled by night with My servants, and when Pharaoh learned of this, he sent men into the cities (المدائن) under his authority as "gatherers" (حاشرِينَ) to muster the people and assemble the crowds, saying to the people: **إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ** — "Indeed these"—referring to the Children of Israel— **لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ** — "are a small band" (26:54). The term **الشرذمة** refers to the small remainder of anything, so describing them as "small" (قليلون) is an emphasis. **وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنا لَغائِظُونَ** — "and they have indeed enraged us" (26:55) by performing deeds that provoke our anger. **وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ** — "and we are a host" united in what we resolve upon, **حاذِرُونَ** — "on our guard" (26:56), wary of the enemy lest they assassinate us or plot against us, even if they are weak and few. The objective of these statements—which were undoubtedly a proclamation from Pharaoh—was to incite the people against them.

**فَأَخْرَجْناهُمْ مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَكُنُوزٍ وَمَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ** — "So We expelled them from gardens and springs, and treasures and a noble station" (26:57-58), which included their lofty palaces and high houses. Since their departure was due to a divine plan (مكر إلهي) triggered by the impulse of superiority and arrogance (الاستكبار) within them, He attributed their expulsion to Himself. **كَذلِكَ** — "So it was" (26:59); meaning the matter was thus. **وَأَوْرَثْناها** — "and We made them an inheritance for" —meaning those gardens, springs, treasures, and the noble station— **بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** — "the Children of Israel," as We destroyed Pharaoh and his hosts and kept the Children of Israel after them, so they became the inheritors.

**فَأَتْبَعُوهُمْ** — "So they pursued them" —meaning they caught up with the Children of Israel— **مُشْرِقِينَ** — "at sunrise" (26:60); meaning entering the time of the sun's rising. **فَلَمَّا تَراءَا الْجَمْعانِ** — "When the two hosts sighted each other" (26:61); meaning they drew near to one another such that each of the two groups—the host of Pharaoh and the host of Moses—saw the other. **قالَ أَصْحابُ مُوسى** — "the companions of Moses said," from among the Children of Israel, in fear and panic: **إِنَّ لَمُدْرَكُونَ** — "‘We are indeed overtaken!’" Pharaoh's soldiers will surely catch us.

**قالَ** — "He said," Moses: **كَلَّا** — "‘No!’" They shall never overtake us. **إِنَّ مَعِي رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ** — "‘Indeed my Lord is with me. He will guide me.’" (26:62). What is meant by this "withness" (المعية) is the withness of protection and support, which is what his Lord promised him when He first sent him and his brother to Pharaoh: **إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما** — "Indeed I am with you both" (20:46). As for the withness of origination and governance (التدبير), Allah, the Glorified, is with Moses and Pharaoh in equal measure. His saying: **سَيَهْدِينِ** — "He will guide me" means: He will direct me to a path by which Pharaoh cannot reach me.

**فأَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى أَنِ اضْرِبْ بِعَصاكَ الْبَحْرَ فَانْفَلَقَ** — "Then We revealed to Moses, [saying], ‘Strike the sea with your staff.’ Thereupon it parted" (26:63). Parting (الانفلاق) is the splitting of a thing and the separation of its parts from one another. **فَكانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ** — "and each part" —meaning a separated piece of water— **كَالطَّوْدِ** — "was like a mountain" **الْعَظِيمِ** — "massive." So Moses and those with him from the Children of Israel entered it.

**وَأَزْلَفْنا ثَمَ** — "And there We brought near" **الْآخَرِينَ** — "the others" (26:64), who were Pharaoh and his hosts. **وَأَنْجَيْنا مُوسى وَمَنْ مَعَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "And We delivered Moses and all those who were with him" (26:65) by maintaining the sea in its state and form until they crossed and exited it. **ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ** — "Then We drowned the rest" (26:66) by closing the sea upon them while they were within its parting.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** — "There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (26:67-68). The apparent context—supported by the context of the stories that follow—is that the referent is the sum total of what was mentioned in the story of Moses: his being sent, his calling Pharaoh and his people, the deliverance of the Children of Israel, and the drowning of Pharaoh and his hosts. In all of that is a sign (آية) indicating His monotheism in Lordship (الربوبية) and the truth of the message for whoever reflects upon it.

His saying: **وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** — "but most of them do not have faith" means that most of those whose story We have mentioned were not believers despite the clarity of the sign indicated. Accordingly, His saying after each of the stories cited in the Surah: **وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** is like drawing a conclusion and applying the example to the one for whom the example is set. It is as if it is said after citing each story: "This is their story containing His sign, yet most of them were not believers, just as most of your people do not believe; so do not grieve over them, for this is the habit of all the nations to whom We sent a messenger who called them to the monotheism of Lordship."

It has been said that the pronoun in "most of them" (أكثرهم) refers to the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the meaning is: "In this story is a sign, yet most of your people do not believe in it." However, this is not without some far-fetchedness.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** — "Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful" has already been explained at the beginning of the Surah.
